coredata.dc:title: Detection and removal of cadmium ion by a neoteric polyethylenimine carbon dots @ lanthanum orthoferrite nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Z, Nawas Sherif
K, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a fluorescent polyethylenimine carbon dots (PEI-CDs) and an adsorbent lanthanum orthoferrite (LaFeO3) are composited as PEI-CDs/LaFeO3 (LFPC), concerning simultaneous detection and removal of cadmium (Cd) ions in the aqueous medium. The fluorescent “Off-On” mechanism is used in detecting cadmium ions and the removal of Cd ions is achieved by the adsorption method. Metal ions selectivity, optimization of the sensor with pH and time, the limit of detection, batch experiments (effect on adsorption by varying pH, initial concentration of metal ions, and adsorbent dosage), and adsorption isotherm studies are carried out in a standardized methodology for effective detection and removal of cadmium ions. The sample's pH is a significant parameter for both detection and removal, here pH 6 is an optimum condition to attain high fluorescent sensitivity and adsorption efficiency. Real sample analysis is performed to check the material's practicability. The synthesized material is characterized by X-ray diffraction, UV‐Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared analysis, scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and energy dispersive X-ray analysis. The lifetime of a fluorophore and zeta potential measurements support the mechanism behind the sensing and the removal of cadmium ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium ion
Fluorescent sensor
Polyethylenimine carbon dot
Adsorption
Lanthanum orthoferrite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample preparation for real sample analysis
Optimization of LFPC for Cd ions detection
Sensitivity of LFPC towards Cd ion
Removal of cadmium ion via adsorption
Effect on adsorption efficiency by pH
Effect on adsorption efficiency by adsorbent dosage
Effect on adsorption efficiency by initial concentration of Cd ion
Detection and removal of cadmium ion in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by RASHTRIYA UCCHATAR SHIKSHA ABHIYAN (RUSA) - New Delhi, India. ( 046/RUSA/MKU/2020-2021 ). The authors thank RUSA for providing financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PXRD pattern of prepared LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite.
HRTEM images of (a) PEI-CDs, (b) LaFeO3, (inset particle size distribution (a–b)), (c) LFPC with d-spacing of LaFeO3 and SEM images of (d) PEI-CDs, (e) LaFeO3 and (f) LFPC nanocomposite.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite and (b) Tauc Plots of LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of prepared LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite (inset: (a) PEI-CDs without illumination, (b) PEI-CDs under UV light irradiation at 360 nm) and (b) Time decay of PEI-CDs and LFPC.
FTIR spectra of prepared LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite.
EDX spectra of LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, and LFPC nanocomposite.

(a) LFPC nanocomposite towards metal ion selection (selectivity of metal ion), (b) selectivity based on the interfering of other metal ions with cadmium ion, (c) pH Optimization of LFPC, (d) Time optimization of LFPC, (e) Sensitivity of Cd ion by increasing the concentration of Cd from 50 nM to 600 nM (inset: zoomed image of sensitivity) and (f) Linear relation between fluorescence intensity with various concentration of Cd.

(a) Effect on adsorption efficiency of Cd ion by pH (Cd Conc. 1 mM, adsorbent dosage 3 g/l), (b) Effect on adsorption efficiency and adsorption capacity of Cd ion by adsorbent dosage (Cd Conc. 1 mM, pH 6), (c) Effect of adsorption efficiency and adsorption capacity of Cd ion by varying initial concentration of Cd (pH 6, Adsorbent dosage 3 g/l) and (d) Cadmium ions adsorption isotherms on LFPC nanocomposite.
Schematic representation of detection and adsorption mechanism.
Zeta potential analysis of LaFeO3, PEI-CDs, Cd ion, and LFPC at pH 6.
Comparing limit of detection of present work with literature.

PEI-CDs/LaFeO3
Perylenetetracarboxylicdiimide
Dansyl-tetrapeptide
CdTe QDs
Zn-MOF
Porphyrin
Dithizone-etched CdTe

50 nM–500 nM
0.1 μM–5.0 μM
0 μM–20 μM
1.3 μM–25 μM
0 μM–12 μM
0.25 μM–2 μM
0.4 μM–15.4 μM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption isotherms parameter of cadmium ion adsorption on LFPC nanocomposite.
Experimental result of real sample analysis.

head.ce:title: Detection and removal of cadmium ion by a neoteric polyethylenimine carbon dots @ lanthanum orthoferrite nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai – 625021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a fluorescent polyethylenimine carbon dots (PEI-CDs) and an adsorbent lanthanum orthoferrite (LaFeO3) are composited as PEI-CDs/LaFeO3 (LFPC), concerning simultaneous detection and removal of cadmium (Cd) ions in the aqueous medium. The fluorescent “Off-On” mechanism is used in detecting cadmium ions and the removal of Cd ions is achieved by the adsorption method. Metal ions selectivity, optimization of the sensor with pH and time, the limit of detection, batch experiments (effect on adsorption by varying pH, initial concentration of metal ions, and adsorbent dosage), and adsorption isotherm studies are carried out in a standardized methodology for effective detection and removal of cadmium ions. The sample's pH is a significant parameter for both detection and removal, here pH 6 is an optimum condition to attain high fluorescent sensitivity and adsorption efficiency. Real sample analysis is performed to check the material's practicability. The synthesized material is characterized by X-ray diffraction, UV‐Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared analysis, scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and energy dispersive X-ray analysis. The lifetime of a fluorophore and zeta potential measurements support the mechanism behind the sensing and the removal of cadmium ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Universally, heavy metal ions cause peril to entire living creatures in the environment, when these are consumed unintentionally. Chiefly, cadmium ions are risky even at very low concentrations. Effects of cadmium disclosures are cardiovascular diseases, cancer mortality, damage to the liver, kidney, central nervous system, neurological and reproductive disorders [1]. Based on this, it is an exigency to detect and remove the heavy metal ions (cadmium) from the aqueous medium.

Among absorption, reflection, and other optical detection methods, the fluorescence detection method has advantages such as fast response time [2,3] and distinctly naked-eye detection of the target heavy metal ion [4]. Recently, Wang et al., developed a highly selective peptide-based fluorescent probe to detect cadmium ions in the aqueous solution [5]. Liu et al., reported a zinc-based metal-organic framework as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of cadmium ions and nitrobenzene [6]. Leena Ranjith et al., detected cadmium ions by water-soluble porphyrin [7]. Here, the semiconductor carbon quantum dots (CDs) is chosen as a fluorophore based on their unique properties such as high photostability, low photobleaching, broad absorption spectra, better fluorescence property, and mainly due to its non-toxicity than other reported materials [8]. These quantum dots are functionalized by polyethylenimine (PEI) as a polymer that helps to enhance the features of carbon dots [9] and to sight target analyte with the aid of amine groups in PEI [10].

Removal of heavy metal ions is carried out mostly by adsorption than chemical precipitation, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, electro dialysis, ultrafiltration, coagulation, etc., in view of the fact that it is economical, uncomplicated, free from residue, and its efficiency [11]. For instance, Naushad et al., established an activated carbon/zirconium oxide composite to remove cadmium ions from an aqueous medium [12]. Chen et al., prepared 3D MnO2-modified biochar-based porous hydrogel for the removal of cadmium and lead ions [13]. In the present work, lanthanum orthoferrite LaFeO3 is used as an adsorbent. This perovskite material is infrequently used in research and provides various advantages over other adsorbents such as its stability, low cost, high surface energy, short intraparticle diffusion time, and magnificent physical, chemical, and magnetic properties [14,15].

Herein, PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 are synthesized by sol-gel method, whereas LFPC nanocomposite is synthesized using a coupling agent, 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), characterized and employed on the simultaneous detection and removal of cadmium ions from the aqueous medium. Furthermore, the Limit of Detection (LOD), percentage of removal efficiency, and adsorption isotherms are calculated for the prepared LFPC nanocomposite in order to notice its fluorescence sensing and adsorption efficiency respectively.

Citric acid (CA), polyethylenimine (PEI) solution, lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (La(NO3)3·6H2O), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)carbodiimide (EDC), phosphate standard for IC–buffer solution, cadmium acetate dihydrate, mercury (II) chloride, lead nitrate, barium chloride dihydrate, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, copper (II) chloride dihydrate, chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate, aluminum chloride anhydrous, cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate, iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate, indium (III) nitrate hydrate, nickel (II) acetate, tin (II) chloride, zinc sulphate heptahydrate and distilled water were purchased as analytical grade from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals private limited, Bangalore, India and utilized without any further purification.

The powder XRD patterns were obtained using a PANalytical X-pert pro diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) operated at a voltage of 40 kV and current of 30 mA. SHIMADZU UV-2450 spectrophotometer was used to record absorption spectra, photoluminescence spectra were obtained from SHIMADZU RF-5301 spectrofluorometer, Fourier transform infrared data was recorded by using SHIMADZU FTIR-8400S, Morphology of the samples was analyzed using scanning electron microscope (TESCAN VEGA3 SBH, 30 kV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN TEM, 200 kV). The elemental composition of the samples was confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), JOBIN YUON Fluorolog-3-11 (Pulsed-Diode source – 340 nm) was utilized to record lifetime measurement of the fluorophore, Zeta potential measurement was done by Malvern Zetasizer MAL1052893 and atomic absorption spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU AA-6300) was meant to determine the concentration of cadmium ions.

PEI-CDs were synthesized via the sol-gel method [10], where citric acid and polyethylenimine solution were the precursors. Initially, 500 μl of polyethylenimine solution and 1 g of citric acid were allowed to fluidify in 25 ml of warm distilled water. The pale-yellow gel was obtained after the evaporation of the solvent in the solution when the solution was treated at 80 °C for a few minutes. Frequently 1000 μl of distilled water was introduced to the gel in order to maintain the heat, as a result, a pale-yellow gel turned to orange-coloured gel. The obtained PEI-CDs gel was purified with ethanol and it was centrifuged to get the precipitate, where the supernatant solution contains ethanol with excess CA and PEI was removed. At last, the precipitate (PEI-CDs) was dissolved in distilled water for further use.

Sol-Gel method was preferred to synthesis lanthanum orthoferrite (LaFeO3). Here, lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (La(NO3)3·6H2O), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) were dissolved in 30 ml of distilled water in a concentration of 0.05 M individually. Then the solution was stirred for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. 0.1 M of citric acid was added to the solution while stirring. Finally, the solution was heated at 80 °C to obtain a brown-coloured gel-like solution, then it was transferred to a watch glass and dried at 100 °C for 6 h, then the sample was grained by mortar and fine powder was calcinated at 600 °C for 2 h. Thus, LaFeO3 was prepared [15,16].

EDC technique was designated to synthesis LFPC nanocomposite. Hither, 0.4 ml of LaFeO3 (2 mg/ml) was blended with 1500 μl of buffer solution in the concentration of 10 mM. Then 100 μl of EDC (10 mM) was introduced to the solution. Thus the solution containing LaFeO3, buffer solution, and EDC was incubated while stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, 0.4 ml of PEI-CDs (1 mg/ml) was added at last and the mixture was stirred for a few minutes. After incubation for several hours, it was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 20 min to remove unwanted surplus reactants to get LFPC [17].

To predict the selectivity of LFPC towards fluorescence sensing response of various metal ions such as mercury, aluminium, barium, cadmium, chromium, copper, manganese, lead, cobalt, iron, indium, nickel, tin, and zinc, sample solutions were prepared in the following way. The stock solutions of the above-mentioned metal ions were prepared with a known concentration of 1 mM. Then 50 μl stock solution of various metals was added individually with 400 μl of prepared LFPC after diluted with 0.5 ml of buffer solution with a concentration of 10 mM [18]. Then the samples were analyzed to select the metal ion. By following the same procedure, more than one metal ions can be added to the LFPC solution to examine the selectivity in presence of other interfering metal ions [17].

At the beginning, the stock solutions of cadmium ion at different concentrations (50 nM, 100 nM, 150 nM, 200 nM, 300 nM, 350 nM, 400 nM, 500 nM, 600 nM) were prepared using cadmium acetate. Then 400 μl of LFPC was diluted with 0.5 ml of buffer solution (10 mM), at last, 50 μl of cadmium ion stock solution at different concentrations was added separately [18]. Later, all the samples were allowed to record the fluorescence spectra.

To perform the batch experiment, the pH of the solution (LFPC with metal ion) was varied by adding 0.1 mol/L of NaOH and HNO3 to adjust the pH value, which was measured with a pH meter [19]. To notice the effect of adsorbent dosage, vary the concentration of adsorbent while synthesizing LFPC and to observe the effects of metal ion concentration on adsorption efficiency, the concentration of metal ions was varied and added to LFPC by following the sample preparation method for sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of the produced LFPC nanocomposite for the detection and removal of cadmium ions was assessed using tap water and river water. We gathered tap water and river water from the neighbourhood. To get rid of the suspended particles, a 0.22 μm filter was used to filter the actual water samples. The samples were then spiked with cadmium ions at doses of 0.5 mM and 1 mM. Then real water samples were added to the LFPC solution using the aforementioned method (sample preparation for sensitivity).

Prepared PEI-CDs, LaFeO3, and LFPC nanocomposite were subjected to Powder X-ray Diffraction (PXRD) analysis as shown in Fig. 1 , to screen its crystallinity and purity [15]. PEI-CDs have a broad diffraction peak at 23.59° corresponding to the (120) plane of highly disordered PEI-CDs, which implies its amorphous nature (JCPDS No. 50-0926) [20,21]. The broad diffraction peak of PEI-CDs also indicates that their particles are relatively small in size [22]. Sharp high-intensity diffraction peak of LaFeO3 at 32.37° corresponds to (112) plane, indicating high crystallinity of about 67 % and purity of LaFeO3 with orthorhombic structure (JCPDS No. 01-074-2203) [15,23]. But, LFPC nanocomposite material exhibits a broad and sharp intense peak of both PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 respectively, other characteristic peaks are not clearly detected due to the homogenous distribution of the materials [24].

The morphology and average particle size of PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 are investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). LaFeO3 possesses block structure [25] and PEI-CDs have quasi-spherical morphology [9] as shown in Fig. 2(a, b). The average particle size of LaFeO3 and PEI-CDs was determined as 24–26 nm and 2–3 nm respectively (inset in Fig. 2(a, b)). Both HRTEM (Fig. 2(a–c)) and SEM (Fig. 2(d–f)) images of LFPC nanocomposite show the presence of both PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 which confirm the successful formation of the nanocomposite.

Fig. 3(a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEI-CDs, LaFeO3, and LFPC nanocomposite. PEI-CDs shows an absorption peak at 353 nm [9], which corresponds to n → π* transition [10]. It shows a blue emission under UV light of 360 nm as in Fig. 4(a) [21]. Thus, blue emission elucidates the dimension of PEI-CDs as lower, because the lower dimensional quantum dot (2–3 nm) emits higher energy light (blue emission) due to a larger band gap [26]. This statement is also supported by its high-frequency absorbance property. LaFeO3 possesses a wide absorption spectrum in the visible region [27]. Hence, LFPC exhibits broad absorption from 300 nm to 700 nm. The band gap energies are calculated using Tauc Plot for PEI-CDs, LaFeO3, and LFPC nanocomposite as 3.12 eV, 1.98 eV, and 3.0 eV respectively (Fig. 3(b)) [16].

Photoluminescence spectra of PEI-CDs, LaFeO3, and LFPC nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 4(a). PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 have emission peaks at 450 nm [10] and about 700 nm [28] respectively. In the PL spectra of LFPC, there are peaks for LaFeO3 and PEI-CDs. However, after preparing the composite the emission intensity of PEI-CDs and LaFeO3 is reduced. Thus the fluorescence of PEI-CDs is quenched when it is composited with LaFeO3. The average lifetime decay of a fluorophore PEI-CDs is determined as 5.21 ns as shown in Fig. 4(b) and it is decreased to 4.8 ns (Fig. 4(b)) when composited with LaFeO3. This downturn of average lifetime supports the fluorescence quenching of PEI-CDs while preparing LFPC nanocomposite [29].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are recorded in the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 to know the functional groups of LFPC nanocomposite as shown in Fig. 5 . In PEI-CDs, peaks observed at 1644 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibration of the NH bond [20]. The peaks at 2957 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 are related to CH bond stretching vibrations [20] and vibration of CH in the aldehyde group [30] respectively. The peaks at 1000 cm−1 and 901 cm−1 are aroused as a result of stretching and bending vibration of the FeO and OFeO bonds respectively [15]. Peak appears at 684 cm−1 by cause of FeO stretching vibrations [24]. The broad and strong peak at 3473 cm−1 indicates the presence of an OH bond [23]. The peaks arise at 2077 cm−1 and 2385 cm−1 due to atmospheric CO and CO2 [31]. The presence of NH bond confirms the interaction between polyethylenimine and the carbon dot that further enhances the properties of the carbon dot [20]. The OH bond is a significant site for adsorption [23].

The elemental distribution of PEI-CDs, LaFeO3, and LFPC nanocomposite is determined by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis, and the obtained result is presented in Fig. 6 . The weight percentage of the elements present in the LFPC nanocomposite are as follows, oxygen - 41.14 %; carbon - 15.66 %; nitrogen - 5.98 %; lanthanum - 29.84 %; and iron - 7.38 %.

Blue emission of polyethylenimine carbon dots was quenched (Off) when lanthanum orthoferrite was composited (LFPC). Various metal ions such as Cd, Cr, Cu, Hg, Mn, Al, Pb, Ba, Co, Fe, In, Ni, Sn, and Zn are incorporated into the material LFPC in order to determine the selectivity towards the metal ion by recovery of the fluorescence emission. The blue emission is recovered (On) at the instant of incorporation of cadmium ions to LFPC. This substantiates that prepared material LFPC has a high sensitivity towards cadmium ions as shown in Fig. 7(a). Accordingly, a unique fluorescence “Off-On” mechanism transpired than the usual “turn off” mechanism. Furthermore, the selectivity of cadmium ions with respect to interference from other metal ions is shown in Fig. 7(b), and it can be noticed that the fluorescence “Off-On” mechanism works well for detecting cadmium ions even when there is interference from some other metal ions. As a result, the fluorescence of the LFPC nanocomposite will be enhanced while detecting cadmium ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of LFPC for Cd ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the sensing ability of LFPC, it is necessary to optimize various experimental conditions such as pH and response time of LFPC towards cadmium ion [32]. Effect of these two factors can affect the performance of the sensor. Hence, the LFPC sensitivity was investigated by varying these parameters, which leads us to know the appropriate pH and response time of the sensor by which cadmium ion can be sensed effectively. The pH of the LFPC solution was changed during LFPC optimization from pH 2 to pH 8 (pH 2, pH 4, pH 6, and pH 8). As seen in Fig. 7(b), pH 6 exhibits a strong fluorescence signal in this case. Hence, pH 6 was chosen as an optimum pH condition (where adsorption of hydrogen ions and precipitation of cadmium were avoided due to low acidic and high basic conditions respectively) to detect cadmium ions effectively. There is no noticeable change in the fluorescence signal while increasing the detection time (Fig. 7(c)), which infers the photostability of the fluorescence sensor. Hence, the sensitivity of LFPC was determined effectively by making use of these optimized experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of LFPC towards Cd ion
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine LFPC sensitivity towards cadmium ion, various concentrations of cadmium ion (50 nM, 100 nM, 150 nM, 200 nM, 300 nM, 350 nM, 400 nM, 500 nM, 600 nM) were incorporated in LFPC solution. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite is gradually raised with the concentration of cadmium ion (Fig. 7(d)). The obtained results were correlated with the relation between the fluorescent enhancement (I/I 0) and concentration of cadmium ion, where I 0 represents the fluorescence intensity before the incorporation of cadmium ion and I represents the fluorescence intensity after the incorporation of cadmium ion. A linear calibration curve is plotted from 50 nM to 600 nM (Fig. 7(e)). It shows a correlation coefficient R2 equal to 0.971 and the lower detection limit was determined as 13.4 nM, using LOD = 3σ/s, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and s represents the slope of the calibration curve [18]. As far as we know, PEI-CDs/LaFeO3 show a very low detection limit of 13.4 nM, indicating a very high sensitivity towards cadmium ions. The limit of detection of LFPC is compared with the literature (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of cadmium ion via adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption efficiency is determined by the removal percentage of cadmium ions by conducting a series of batch experiments [19,36]. Three experiments are regulated to know the efficiency of adsorption. Firstly, varying the pH of a solution having constant adsorbent dosage and Cd ion concentration. Secondly, varying the adsorbent dosage, where the concentration of Cd ion and pH of the solution is kept constant. Thirdly, the initial concentration of cadmium ions is changed while maintaining a constant pH and adsorbent dose. The concentration of cadmium ions is determined by atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS).

The removal percentage of cadmium ions will be higher due to the large number of vacant adsorbent sites for adsorbate (cadmium ion). The adsorption efficiency and adsorption capacity of adsorbent can be calculated by following equations [15,19],
Adsorption efficiency
Removal %
=
C 0
−
C 1
C 0
× 100
Adsorption capacity
q e
=
C 0
−
C 1
×
V W
mg / g
where C 0 denotes the known initial concentration of metal ion that is incorporated into the material, C 1 denotes the final concentration of metal ion observed from atomic absorption spectroscopy after the adsorption of the metal ion, V is the volume of working solution in litres and W represents the mass of adsorbent in gram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on adsorption efficiency by pH
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption efficiency or removal percentage of metal ion (Cd) elementarily depends on the pH of the solution. With unsuitable pH, one can erroneously predict the adsorption efficiency of the metal ion. The pH of the solution affects the adsorption active site of an adsorbent. Here, to determine suitable pH, the effect of pH was analyzed by varying from 2 to 8 (pH) of the solution having adsorbent dosage (3 g/l) and 1 mM concentration of cadmium ion. As a result, it is noticed that the removal efficiency of cadmium ion raised from 97.1 % to 97.9 % with an increase in pH from 2 to 6 respectively and the removal efficiency decreased at pH 8 as shown in Fig. 8(a). Hydrogen ions are mostly get adsorbed on the LFPC nanocomposite at low pH i.e., the acidic region leads to low adsorption efficiency [37]. Above pH 6, there is a no much more adsorption efficiency. At pH 8, adsorption efficiency gradually decreases due to drastic precipitation of Cd ion on basic region simply on high pH value [19]. Thus pH 6 was determined and the other two experiments (i.e.,) the effect on adsorption efficiency by adsorbent dosage and the effect on adsorption efficiency by initial concentration of Cd ion were conducted with pH 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on adsorption efficiency by adsorbent dosage
ce:section.ce:para: To observe the adsorption efficiency on the effect of adsorbent dosage, LFPC nanocomposites are made by altering the adsorbent, LaFeO3 concentration (5 g/l, 10 g/l, 15 g/l, and 20 g/l), while maintaining pH 6 and 1 mM concentration of Cd ion. The removal efficiency increases from 97.03 % to 98 % and adsorption capacity decreases from 0.103 mg/g to 0.026 mg/g as the adsorbent dosage increases from 5 g/l to 20 g/l as shown in Fig. 8(b). It is inferred that the surface area of an adsorbent increases as the dosage of an adsorbent increases, thus resulting in a high removal efficiency of the metal ion [19]. The prepared LFPC nanocomposite have high adsorption efficiency by increasing the adsorbent dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on adsorption efficiency by initial concentration of Cd ion
ce:section.ce:para: On comparing the concentration of cadmium ion, its removal efficiency is noticed. Working with solutions of known concentration (C0) of cadmium ion (1 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, and 15 mM) incorporated into LFPC nanocomposite has an adsorbent dosage of 3 g/l and pH of the solution is maintained at 6. After adsorption, the final concentration (C1) of the cadmium ion in the working solution is noticed. Thus, the removal efficiency decreases from 98.2 % to 71.9 % and adsorption capacity increases from 0.174 mg/g to 2.569 mg/g on increasing the concentration of cadmium ion, due to lack of adsorption vacant site on the adsorbent [37] (Fig. 8(c)).

An effective equilibrium adsorption isotherm models such as Langmuir and Freundlich models were applied to determine the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent [38]. Simply, adsorption capacity reveals the amount of adsorbate that is adsorbed on the adsorbent [19]. Hence, these two models (Langmuir and Freundlich) were used to know adsorption characteristics. The following equations represent the Langmuir (A) and Freundlich (B) models respectively [39,40].
(A)
q e
=
Q m
K L
C e
/ 1 +
K L
C e
(B)
q e
=
K F
C e
1 / n

The adsorption parameters can be determined by the linear form of Langmuir (C) and Freundlich (D) models [39] which are represented as follows,
(C)
1 /
q e
=
1 /
K L
Q m
∙ 1 /
C e
+ 1 /
Q m
(D)
Log
q e
= log
K F
+ 1 / n
log
C e
where q
e is the amount of adsorbate adsorbed on the adsorbent (mg/g), C
e is the equilibrium concentration of adsorbate in the solution (mg/l), Q
m is the maximum amount of adsorbate adsorbed on the adsorbent, K
L represents Langmuir constant which is the enthalpy of the adsorption, K
F represents Freundlich constant and 1/n is the adsorption intensity.

Based on experimental data (batch experiments), adsorption parameters are analyzed by both Langmuir and Freundlich models, and the values are given in Table 2 . Here, the cadmium ion adsorption on LFPC is multilayer adsorption [41], since Freundlich adsorption isotherm has a higher linear regression coefficient than Langmuir isotherm (Fig. 8(d)). Thus, physical adsorption takes place by removing the cadmium ion from the contaminated water [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and removal of cadmium ion in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of LFPC nanocomposite in identifying and eliminating cadmium ions from actual samples was evaluated, and the experimental outcomes are shown in Table 3 . Tap water and river water were collected, filtered, and spiked with the known concentration of cadmium ion and allowed to record their fluorescence spectra and their final concentration to know their fluorescence recovery (turn ON) and its adsorption efficiency respectively. The fluorescence recovery for 0.5 mM and 1 mM tap water is around 92.1 % and 99.2 %, respectively, and their adsorption efficiency is estimated as 99.18 % and 93.3 %, respectively. Fluorescence recovery for river water of concentrations of 0.5 and 1 mM is approximately 83.3 % and 93.9 %, respectively and their adsorption efficiency is estimated as 98.9 % and 93.4 %, respectively. As a result, the fluorescence recovery percentage rises and adsorption efficiency marginally declines while increasing the concentration of cadmium ions.

In this study, the LFPC nanocomposite has been successfully developed for the simultaneous detection and removal of cadmium ions. The average particle size of LaFeO3 and PEI-CDs was determined as 24–26 nm and 2–3 nm respectively using HRTEM. This was supported by the high-frequency absorption of the nanocomposite. The size, band gap, and emission peak of the material are correlated with the quantum confinement effect. In lifetime decay studies, the downturn of the average lifetime (5.21 ns to 4.8 ns) while nanocompositing PEI-CDs with LaFeO3 supports the fluorescence quenching of PEI-CDs. The vibrational and elemental analysis confirmed the formation of the nanocomposite. Detection and removal of cadmium are based on the fluorescent ‘off-on’ mechanism and adsorption mechanism respectively. The sensitivity of LFPC was determined by making use of optimized pH (pH 6) and response time (30 min) towards the cadmium ions. The prepared LFPC nanocomposite shows very high sensitivity towards cadmium ion i.e., it can sense 13.4 nM of cadmium ion in water.

The effects on the adsorption efficiency by pH, adsorbent dosage, and initial concentration of Cd ion were studied with optimum pH 6, the LFPC has high adsorption efficiency by increasing adsorbent dosage. The initial concentration of Cd ion plays a role in the adsorption efficiency; as the concentration of cadmium ion increases, removal efficiency decreases. The physical adsorption was also confirmed by the isotherm studies which revealed that multilayer adsorption was carried out on adsorbing cadmium ions. The detection and removal of Cd ion in real samples such as tap water, and river water around us were done. The adsorption efficiency of 0.5 mM and 1 mM of tap water was found as 99.2 % and 93.3 % respectively and their fluorescence recovery was determined as 92.1 % and 99.2 %. The adsorption efficiency of 0.5 mM and 1 mM river water is found as 98.9 % and 93.4 % respectively and their fluorescence recovery was calculated as 83.3 % and 93.9 %. Thus the prepared LFPC nanocomposite detected and removed cadmium ions efficiently in the aqueous medium.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by RASHTRIYA UCCHATAR SHIKSHA ABHIYAN (RUSA) - New Delhi, India. (046/RUSA/MKU/2020-2021). The authors thank RUSA for providing financial support.
